narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and personal factors from past students. The model estimated a 95.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is an extremely high risk—far above what admissions teams consider acceptable for approval. This led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed and contribute positively after graduation. The decision hinged most heavily on your academic decile rankings from school years 1 and 3, along with your LSAT score, which together painted a picture of underperformance relative to peers who typically pass. Your Year 3 decile ranking of 1—well below the average of about 5.5—was the single biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model that your later academic trajectory didn't build the strong foundation needed for bar success. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2, compared to an average around 5.7, reinforced this concern by highlighting early struggles that often persist. Your LSAT score of 28, much lower than the typical 36.7 among successful students, added further weight toward the failure prediction, as it indicates challenges with the analytical skills crucial for law. Other aspects, like your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 (below the 3.21 average) and full-time student status—which is common among 93% of applicants—played smaller roles and weren't enough to offset these red flags. In contrast to students who get approved, who usually rank in higher deciles and score above 35 on the LSAT, your profile aligns more closely with those historically predicted to struggle on the bar. While factors like your family income bracket didn't sway things much, the model saw your overall academic signals as mismatched with bar passers. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim for at least 35 or higher, bolstering your GPA through post-bac courses or targeted tutoring, and building a stronger academic record in any upcoming terms—these steps could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor for other schools. You're capable of turning this around with deliberate effort, and many applicants in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these core areas head-on.
